{{commons category|Category:Kattamine-jinja (Miya, Zao)}} [[JA:刈田嶺神社 (蔵王町宮)]] {{wikidata link|Q11396254}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Kattamine Shrine |Image = [[File:Kattamine jinja Haiden.JPG|270px|Katamine Shrine Worship Hall]]
Harmony Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874}}Miyaza Baba 1 |Position = {{Coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shakaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin University}}) Ronsha
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation ={{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}Ouomi Shrine Yushin Legend |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternative name = Shiratori Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=January 14th|Q2257|WD=Q2257}}, {{ill|lt=March 19th|Q2424|WD=Q2424}}, {{ill|lt=September 9th|Q2856|WD=Q2856}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Katamine Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Kattamine jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Kattamine jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Hall]]Kattamine Shrine is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874}}. It is a Shikinai shrine named {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}," and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is the largest shrine in the county, and is the general guardian of Katta County and the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Date clan|Q1151061|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date family}} vassals {{ill|lt=Katakura clan|Q15149847|WD=Q15149847|lt=Katakura family}} ({{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}} lord). Another name is ``Shiratori Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} In {{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, where our company is located, and in the neighboring {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}}, there is a ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=swan|1=UNKNOWN}} faith,'' and it is said that there are many swan {{ill|lt=votive tablets|Q687288|WD=Q687288}} dedicated to our company.[http://www.dokitan.com/sitei/25.html Kattamine Shrine Ema] (Zao Town Board of Education)[https://www.town.shibata.miyagi.jp/index.cfm/81,27038,154,279,html Shiratori Incident and the Shibata Family] (Shibata Town)``Murata Town History''. There is also a group of ancient Shiratori monuments on the grounds. For the relationship between Shiratori and Takeru Nihon, please refer to the corresponding article. === History === The auspicious origins of Kattamine ShrineRyuho Jitsumasa Yasuon Kunshu 1716 "Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Origin" (Reprinted in "Zao Town History Materials Part II" by Zao Town History Compilation Committee 1989) b>Tosayfupersons haveri'' and ``舊TOmineノShrineto號su''. At that time, Mt. Aoma was called ``Okattayama,'' and it is the origin of Kattamine Shrine. At the Zuishinmon Gate ({{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}}10 ​​(1827)), there is a vertical plaque that reads ``Mine Shrine.'' {{ill|lt=Nara Period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}}{{ill|lt=Yoro|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} From the Shiroishi side where the county residence of Katta District, founded in 722, is located. The view of Mt. Aoma is the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=twin-eared peak|1=UNKNOWN}} of ``Mt. Akera'' (820m) and ``Mt. Aoma'' (799m). It closely resembles {{ill|lt=Mt. Tsukuba|Q1754619|WD=Q1754619}} (Mt. Nyotai 877m, Mt. Nantai 871m), and the oldest belief in Kattamine deity is Mt. Aoma. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mt. Shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} Nobumitsu Katakura, 1957, “Okatta Sanroku Essays,” Sendai Local Studies Vol. 17, No. 1, pp. 52-58.In {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}} and {{ill|lt=Katsuta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, there is a ``Swan Legend'' related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}}. There are several shrines that remain, and according to old legend, when Takeru Takeru, the second son of Emperor Keiko, was on his eastern expedition, Takeru moved to Kumabono in Seishu. She died, turned into a swan and flew away, and enshrined Shiratori Myojin where she landed, or {{ill|lt=Emperor Yomei|Q349281|WD=Q349281}}'s daughter {{ill|lt=Anahobe Manjin Princess. It is said that|Q11597250|WD=Q11597250}} (mother of {{ill|lt=Prince Shotoku|Q263972|WD=Q263972}}) died in this area, turned into a swan, and the local people enshrined a shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakuma Dogan|1=UNKNOWN}}1719 "Ou Kansekimon Roshi" Volume 4. There is a legend that the second son of Japan Takeru, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}, ordered the lord of Katta County to build a shrine on the western mountain of Katta, where a swan perched..In {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, in the 20th year (801) of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}, {{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}} invaded Ezo. When they prayed for the pacification of the Toi at the Nishinomiya Shrine (enshrined deity: Nihon Takeru) during the logging, Prince Myonen Daito ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ani Ryutame|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Lord Bangu ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mothere|1=UNKNOWN}}) surrendered, and the remnants were slaughtered and released into exile. At this time, the shogun transferred Mine no Kami to the hall of the West Palace, where it was enshrined as a shrine. A small fable enshrining a wooden statue of Yakushi was built in the ruins of the Mine Shrine, and this led to the mountain being named ``Yakushigatake.''{{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} caused by the Mutsu Province Earthquake on May 26, 869, Karitamine was appointed as the Junior Fourth Rank Lower on December 8 of the same year, and again on December 25. In Sakuma Togan's 1719 ``Ou Kansekimon Roshi,'' the rank of the lower deity of Jushimi was super-rank, questioning the fact that Karita Mine no Kami was super-rank in December 1997, and suggested that there may have been some omission of facts during this time. (``{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}''), and at the time, this great earthquake and tsunami was considered to be a ``sacred ritual caused by the wonders of the volcano.'' This is said to be because it was interpreted as being caused by the ``wrath of the mountain god.''Togo Yoshida, 1906, ``The 11th year of Jokan, the earthquake and flood of Mutsufu Castle,'' Historical Geography, Vol. 8, No. 1, pp. 1-8. It is difficult to think that Mt. Aoma exhibited some kind of divine power (volcanic activity), and the "Karita Mine" mentioned in "{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}" is attached to the summit of Zao. It seems to be referring to the vicinity of the nearby crater.Junichi Aihara 2018 "Tagajo and Jogan Tsunami" Archeology Magazine Vol. 101 No. 1, Archaeological Society of Japan, pp. 1-53. . ``Zao Daigongen Shrine'' (Okumiya) of Kattamine Shrine, which is currently enshrined on Mt. Kattadake, and later ``Zao Daigongen Otabigu Shrine'' (Satomiya Shrine) in Togatta, are not only the enshrined deity ``Zao Daigongen,'' but also ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ten Water God|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokusui God|1=UNKNOWN}}, which are positioned as the gods in charge of the water of heaven and earth throughout Mutsu Province.At the end of the Heian period, it was under the protection of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|Q3082851|WD=Q3082851}}, {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, and {{ill|lt=Oshu Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942}}, but with the fall of the Oshu Fujiwara clan, it lost its protectors, and it is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo left it ``scattered left and right with rubble, with nowhere to put its feet.''. During the year {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}}, the chief priest's distant grandson Shokan Sato had the will to restore the area, and since Nishinomiya belonged to the ancient path and was no longer accessible to local residents, he moved Nishinomiya to the northwest of Miyamura (current location). By the end of {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039}}, it was once again in ruins, and local residents were downsizing it and rebuilding it as a small shrine. {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} In 1600, Date Masamune attacked Shiroishi Castle and made Katakura Kagetsuna the lord of Shiroishi Castle. Kagetsuna designated Miyarenzo-ji Temple as his Bettoji Temple, and decided to rebuild a major shrine from the devastation of the shrine. In 1611, the second generation, Shigenaga, carried out the wishes of his predecessor, cleaning out the old shrine and renovating the various halls. He appointed Kanzai as a Sengu doshi, gave him 20 koku of temple territory, and made Bettoji Renzo-ji Temple the Katakura family's temple for the blessings of disaster relief.The current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}}3 ({{ill|lt=1718|Q6899|WD=Q6899}}) by {{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}}lord {{ill|lt=Katakura Murakyu|Q6375040|WD=Q6375040}}. ``Records of the Yamaga Clan Shinto Priests'' (formerly owned by Tomomi Sato, copied by Nobumitsu Katakura, reprinted in ``Zao Town History Materials Volume II'')) states, ``In 1988, the lord of the feudal lord, Katakura Ujimura Kyushu, built the main shrine's worship hall Nagatoko torii, and was subordinated by Lord Yoshimura, the Sakonoe Lieutenant General of the Gobukaza.. === History of company name === The company names are ``Reine no Shrine'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716) and ``Shiratori Myojin'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716). (2010), ``Katta{{Ruby|Shrine|Miya}}'' (by local people, this is called ``Kattamiya Shrine''.There is a temple called Takarachiyama Renzo-ji Temple on the south side of the shrine, and according to both legends, it is mentioned in the Shinmecho. The Karutamine Shrine in Tokoro is not actually the shrine of this shrine.On the other hand, there are doubts about the Chokan 11th year of the reign of Karutamine. It is called Mt. Fubo, and since there is Zao Gongen Shrine on the mountain, it is called Zao Dake. }} Lists the volcanic activity in Zao in 1999 (Ou Kanseimon Roshi, 1719), Kattamine Shrine (1719). ``Hounai Meisekishi'' (1741), ``Katamine Shrine'' (``Hunai Fudoki'', 1772), ``Shoichii Shiratori Daimyo Shrine'' (``Engishiki Jinmyocho Nisōdai Kō Katta District Ichizamine Shrine Nigozako. ``Okoha Okatta Yamanouchi Gojinza Ari no Koro,'' ``Yasunaga Fudoki Goyosho Deri'' (1777), June 10, 1872, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} It was listed as a local shrine, and on March 1, 1907, it was designated as a Shinto shrine and a designated shrine, and on November 9, 1928, in the Godaiten, it was listed as a prefectural shrine.Due to the defeat in the {{ill|lt=Boshin War|Q309184|WD=Q309184}}, in 1870, the former lord of Shiroishi Castle, Katakura Kojuro Kuninori, ordered over 150 of his former vassals to join him. He moved to Horobetsu District, Shunkoku, and enshrined a branch spirit of Japan Takeru, the deity enshrined at Kattamine Shrine, the general guardian deity of the Katakura clan, and enshrined it at the local Myoken Inari Shrine, making it the guardian deity of the development of Horobetsu District. In 1871, a new shrine building was erected at Hama-Nanaju, Horobetsu Village, and the company name was changed to {{ill|lt=Katta Shrine (Noboribetsu City)|Q17226399|WD=Q17226399|lt=Katta Shrine}}. ===Shrine=== *Main shrine *Temple of worship *Torii *Zuishinmon *Belfry *Kagura stage === Surroundings === * {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981}} === Shrine of the same name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Alias |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Prefecture}} !{{ill|lt=gun|Q517267|WD=Q517267}} !{{ill|lt=town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478}} !colspan="2" |district !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|lt=Miya

gun}} | |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Range|Q167951|WD=Q167951|lt=Zao}}・Mt. Katta (sengu from Mt. Kumano) |A crater that displays divine power around the summit |{{Wiki coordinates|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Mt. Katta)|Q65249271|WD=Q65249271|lt=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Okumiya'''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|lt=Kura

!{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mount Aoma|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mount Okarita) |Mine Shrine, which enshrines the mountain body (Okattayama), is enshrined with Shiratori Shrine (Nihon Takeru) in Nishiyama |{{Wiki coordinates|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyojin)}} !Katamine Shrine |'''Shiratori Daimyojin'''' |} {{List of coordinates|section=Shrine of the same name}} Additionally, on the Yamagata Prefecture side, there are Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine) (Shimotakarazawa, Yamagata City), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hango, Ishidaka, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, Kaminoyama City). === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation ={{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}Ouomi Shrine Yushin Legend |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternative name = Shiratori Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=January 14th|Q2257|WD=Q2257}}, {{ill|lt=March 19th|Q2424|WD=Q2424}}, {{ill|lt=September 9th|Q2856|WD=Q2856}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Katamine Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Kattamine jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Kattamine jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Hall]]Kattamine Shrine is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874}}. It is a Shikinai shrine named {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}," and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is the largest shrine in the county, and is the general guardian of Katta County and the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Date clan|Q1151061|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date family}} vassals {{ill|lt=Katakura clan|Q15149847|WD=Q15149847|lt=Katakura family}} ({{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}} lord). Another name is ``Shiratori Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} In {{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, where our company is located, and in the neighboring {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}}, there is a ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=swan|1=UNKNOWN}} faith,'' and it is said that there are many swan {{ill|lt=votive tablets|Q687288|WD=Q687288}} dedicated to our company.[http://www.dokitan.com/sitei/25.html Kattamine Shrine Ema] (Zao Town Board of Education)[https://www.town.shibata.miyagi.jp/index.cfm/81,27038,154,279,html Shiratori Incident and the Shibata Family] (Shibata Town)``Murata Town History''. There is also a group of ancient Shiratori monuments on the grounds. For the relationship between Shiratori and Takeru Nihon, please refer to the corresponding article. === History === The auspicious origins of Kattamine ShrineRyuho Jitsumasa Yasuon Kunshu 1716 "Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Origin" (Reprinted in "Zao Town History Materials Part II" by Zao Town History Compilation Committee 1989) b>Tosayfupersons haveri'' and ``舊TOmineノShrineto號su''. At that time, Mt. Aoma was called ``Okattayama,'' and it is the origin of Kattamine Shrine. At the Zuishinmon Gate ({{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}}10 ​​(1827)), there is a vertical plaque that reads ``Mine Shrine.'' {{ill|lt=Nara Period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}}{{ill|lt=Yoro|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} From the Shiroishi side where the county residence of Katta District, founded in 722, is located. The view of Mt. Aoma is the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=twin-eared peak|1=UNKNOWN}} of ``Mt. Akera'' (820m) and ``Mt. Aoma'' (799m). It closely resembles {{ill|lt=Mt. Tsukuba|Q1754619|WD=Q1754619}} (Mt. Nyotai 877m, Mt. Nantai 871m), and the oldest belief in Kattamine deity is Mt. Aoma. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mt. Shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} Nobumitsu Katakura, 1957, “Okatta Sanroku Essays,” Sendai Local Studies Vol. 17, No. 1, pp. 52-58.In {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}} and {{ill|lt=Katsuta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, there is a ``Swan Legend'' related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}}. There are several shrines that remain, and according to old legend, when Takeru Takeru, the second son of Emperor Keiko, was on his eastern expedition, Takeru moved to Kumabono in Seishu. She died, turned into a swan and flew away, and enshrined Shiratori Myojin where she landed, or {{ill|lt=Emperor Yomei|Q349281|WD=Q349281}}'s daughter {{ill|lt=Anahobe Manjin Princess. It is said that|Q11597250|WD=Q11597250}} (mother of {{ill|lt=Prince Shotoku|Q263972|WD=Q263972}}) died in this area, turned into a swan, and the local people enshrined a shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakuma Dogan|1=UNKNOWN}}1719 "Ou Kansekimon Roshi" Volume 4. There is a legend that the second son of Japan Takeru, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}, ordered the lord of Katta County to build a shrine on the western mountain of Katta, where a swan perched..In {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, in the 20th year (801) of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}, {{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}} invaded Ezo. When they prayed for the pacification of the Toi at the Nishinomiya Shrine (enshrined deity: Nihon Takeru) during the logging, Prince Myonen Daito ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ani Ryutame|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Lord Bangu ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mothere|1=UNKNOWN}}) surrendered, and the remnants were slaughtered and released into exile. At this time, the shogun transferred Mine no Kami to the hall of the West Palace, where it was enshrined as a shrine. A small fable enshrining a wooden statue of Yakushi was built in the ruins of the Mine Shrine, and this led to the mountain being named ``Yakushigatake.''{{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} caused by the Mutsu Province Earthquake on May 26, 869, Karitamine was appointed as the Junior Fourth Rank Lower on December 8 of the same year, and again on December 25. In Sakuma Togan's 1719 ``Ou Kansekimon Roshi,'' the rank of the lower deity of Jushimi was super-rank, questioning the fact that Karita Mine no Kami was super-rank in December 1997, and suggested that there may have been some omission of facts during this time. (``{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}''), and at the time, this great earthquake and tsunami was considered to be a ``sacred ritual caused by the wonders of the volcano.'' This is said to be because it was interpreted as being caused by the ``wrath of the mountain god.''Togo Yoshida, 1906, ``The 11th year of Jokan, the earthquake and flood of Mutsufu Castle,'' Historical Geography, Vol. 8, No. 1, pp. 1-8. It is difficult to think that Mt. Aoma exhibited some kind of divine power (volcanic activity), and the "Karita Mine" mentioned in "{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}" is attached to the summit of Zao. It seems to be referring to the vicinity of the nearby crater.Junichi Aihara 2018 "Tagajo and Jogan Tsunami" Archeology Magazine Vol. 101 No. 1, Archaeological Society of Japan, pp. 1-53. . ``Zao Daigongen Shrine'' (Okumiya) of Kattamine Shrine, which is currently enshrined on Mt. Kattadake, and later ``Zao Daigongen Otabigu Shrine'' (Satomiya Shrine) in Togatta, are not only the enshrined deity ``Zao Daigongen,'' but also ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ten Water God|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokusui God|1=UNKNOWN}}, which are positioned as the gods in charge of the water of heaven and earth throughout Mutsu Province.At the end of the Heian period, it was under the protection of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|Q3082851|WD=Q3082851}}, {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, and {{ill|lt=Oshu Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942}}, but with the fall of the Oshu Fujiwara clan, it lost its protectors, and it is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo left it ``scattered left and right with rubble, with nowhere to put its feet.''. During the year {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}}, the chief priest's distant grandson Shokan Sato had the will to restore the area, and since Nishinomiya belonged to the ancient path and was no longer accessible to local residents, he moved Nishinomiya to the northwest of Miyamura (current location). By the end of {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039}}, it was once again in ruins, and local residents were downsizing it and rebuilding it as a small shrine. {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} In 1600, Date Masamune attacked Shiroishi Castle and made Katakura Kagetsuna the lord of Shiroishi Castle. Kagetsuna designated Miyarenzo-ji Temple as his Bettoji Temple, and decided to rebuild a major shrine from the devastation of the shrine. In 1611, the second generation, Shigenaga, carried out the wishes of his predecessor, cleaning out the old shrine and renovating the various halls. He appointed Kanzai as a Sengu doshi, gave him 20 koku of temple territory, and made Bettoji Renzo-ji Temple the Katakura family's temple for the blessings of disaster relief.The current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}}3 ({{ill|lt=1718|Q6899|WD=Q6899}}) by {{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}}lord {{ill|lt=Katakura Murakyu|Q6375040|WD=Q6375040}}. ``Records of the Yamaga Clan Shinto Priests'' (formerly owned by Tomomi Sato, copied by Nobumitsu Katakura, reprinted in ``Zao Town History Materials Volume II'')) states, ``In 1988, the lord of the feudal lord, Katakura Ujimura Kyushu, built the main shrine's worship hall Nagatoko torii, and was subordinated by Lord Yoshimura, the Sakonoe Lieutenant General of the Gobukaza.. === History of company name === The company names are ``Reine no Shrine'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716) and ``Shiratori Myojin'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716). (2010), ``Katta{{Ruby|Shrine|Miya}}'' (by local people, this is called ``Kattamiya Shrine''.There is a temple called Takarachiyama Renzo-ji Temple on the south side of the shrine, and according to both legends, it is mentioned in the Shinmecho. The Karutamine Shrine in Tokoro is not actually the shrine of this shrine.On the other hand, there are doubts about the Chokan 11th year of the reign of Karutamine. It is called Mt. Fubo, and since there is Zao Gongen Shrine on the mountain, it is called Zao Dake. }} Lists the volcanic activity in Zao in 1999 (Ou Kanseimon Roshi, 1719), Kattamine Shrine (1719). ``Hounai Meisekishi'' (1741), ``Katamine Shrine'' (``Hunai Fudoki'', 1772), ``Shoichii Shiratori Daimyo Shrine'' (``Engishiki Jinmyocho Nisōdai Kō Katta District Ichizamine Shrine Nigozako. ``Okoha Okatta Yamanouchi Gojinza Ari no Koro,'' ``Yasunaga Fudoki Goyosho Deri'' (1777), June 10, 1872, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} It was listed as a local shrine, and on March 1, 1907, it was designated as a Shinto shrine and a designated shrine, and on November 9, 1928, in the Godaiten, it was listed as a prefectural shrine.Due to the defeat in the {{ill|lt=Boshin War|Q309184|WD=Q309184}}, in 1870, the former lord of Shiroishi Castle, Katakura Kojuro Kuninori, ordered over 150 of his former vassals to join him. He moved to Horobetsu District, Shunkoku, and enshrined a branch spirit of Japan Takeru, the deity enshrined at Kattamine Shrine, the general guardian deity of the Katakura clan, and enshrined it at the local Myoken Inari Shrine, making it the guardian deity of the development of Horobetsu District. In 1871, a new shrine building was erected at Hama-Nanaju, Horobetsu Village, and the company name was changed to {{ill|lt=Katta Shrine (Noboribetsu City)|Q17226399|WD=Q17226399|lt=Katta Shrine}}. ===Shrine=== *Main shrine *Temple of worship *Torii *Zuishinmon *Belfry *Kagura stage === Surroundings === * {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981}} === Shrine of the same name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Alias |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Prefecture}} !{{ill|lt=gun|Q517267|WD=Q517267}} !{{ill|lt=town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478}} !colspan="2" |district !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|lt=Miya

Daimyojin)}} !Katamine Shrine |'''Shiratori Daimyojin'''' |} {{List of coordinates|section=Shrine of the same name}} Additionally, on the Yamagata Prefecture side, there are Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine) (Shimotakarazawa, Yamagata City), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hango, Ishidaka, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, Kaminoyama City). === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === {{commons category|Category:Kattamine-jinja (Miya, Zao)}} [[JA:刈田嶺神社 (蔵王町宮)]] {{wikidata link|Q11396254}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Kattamine Shrine |Image = [[File:Kattamine jinja Haiden.JPG|270px|Katamine Shrine Worship Hall]]
Harmony Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874}}Miyaza Baba 1 |Position = {{Coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shakaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin University}}) Ronsha
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation ={{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}Ouomi Shrine Yushin Legend |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternative name = Shiratori Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=January 14th|Q2257|WD=Q2257}}, {{ill|lt=March 19th|Q2424|WD=Q2424}}, {{ill|lt=September 9th|Q2856|WD=Q2856}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Katamine Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Kattamine jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Kattamine jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Hall]]Kattamine Shrine is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874}}. It is a Shikinai shrine named {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}," and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is the largest shrine in the county, and is the general guardian of Katta County and the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Date clan|Q1151061|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date family}} vassals {{ill|lt=Katakura clan|Q15149847|WD=Q15149847|lt=Katakura family}} ({{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}} lord). Another name is ``Shiratori Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} In {{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, where our company is located, and in the neighboring {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}}, there is a ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=swan|1=UNKNOWN}} faith,'' and it is said that there are many swan {{ill|lt=votive tablets|Q687288|WD=Q687288}} dedicated to our company.[http://www.dokitan.com/sitei/25.html Kattamine Shrine Ema] (Zao Town Board of Education)[https://www.town.shibata.miyagi.jp/index.cfm/81,27038,154,279,html Shiratori Incident and the Shibata Family] (Shibata Town)``Murata Town History''. There is also a group of ancient Shiratori monuments on the grounds. For the relationship between Shiratori and Takeru Nihon, please refer to the corresponding article. === History === The auspicious origins of Kattamine ShrineRyuho Jitsumasa Yasuon Kunshu 1716 "Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Origin" (Reprinted in "Zao Town History Materials Part II" by Zao Town History Compilation Committee 1989) b>Tosayfupersons haveri'' and ``舊TOmineノShrineto號su''. At that time, Mt. Aoma was called ``Okattayama,'' and it is the origin of Kattamine Shrine. At the Zuishinmon Gate ({{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}}10 ​​(1827)), there is a vertical plaque that reads ``Mine Shrine.'' {{ill|lt=Nara Period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}}{{ill|lt=Yoro|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} From the Shiroishi side where the county residence of Katta District, founded in 722, is located. The view of Mt. Aoma is the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=twin-eared peak|1=UNKNOWN}} of ``Mt. Akera'' (820m) and ``Mt. Aoma'' (799m). It closely resembles {{ill|lt=Mt. Tsukuba|Q1754619|WD=Q1754619}} (Mt. Nyotai 877m, Mt. Nantai 871m), and the oldest belief in Kattamine deity is Mt. Aoma. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mt. Shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} Nobumitsu Katakura, 1957, “Okatta Sanroku Essays,” Sendai Local Studies Vol. 17, No. 1, pp. 52-58.In {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}} and {{ill|lt=Katsuta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, there is a ``Swan Legend'' related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}}. There are several shrines that remain, and according to old legend, when Takeru Takeru, the second son of Emperor Keiko, was on his eastern expedition, Takeru moved to Kumabono in Seishu. She died, turned into a swan and flew away, and enshrined Shiratori Myojin where she landed, or {{ill|lt=Emperor Yomei|Q349281|WD=Q349281}}'s daughter {{ill|lt=Anahobe Manjin Princess. It is said that|Q11597250|WD=Q11597250}} (mother of {{ill|lt=Prince Shotoku|Q263972|WD=Q263972}}) died in this area, turned into a swan, and the local people enshrined a shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakuma Dogan|1=UNKNOWN}}1719 "Ou Kansekimon Roshi" Volume 4. There is a legend that the second son of Japan Takeru, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}, ordered the lord of Katta County to build a shrine on the western mountain of Katta, where a swan perched..In {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, in the 20th year (801) of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}, {{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}} invaded Ezo. When they prayed for the pacification of the Toi at the Nishinomiya Shrine (enshrined deity: Nihon Takeru) during the logging, Prince Myonen Daito ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ani Ryutame|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Lord Bangu ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mothere|1=UNKNOWN}}) surrendered, and the remnants were slaughtered and released into exile. At this time, the shogun transferred Mine no Kami to the hall of the West Palace, where it was enshrined as a shrine. A small fable enshrining a wooden statue of Yakushi was built in the ruins of the Mine Shrine, and this led to the mountain being named ``Yakushigatake.''{{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} caused by the Mutsu Province Earthquake on May 26, 869, Karitamine was appointed as the Junior Fourth Rank Lower on December 8 of the same year, and again on December 25. In Sakuma Togan's 1719 ``Ou Kansekimon Roshi,'' the rank of the lower deity of Jushimi was super-rank, questioning the fact that Karita Mine no Kami was super-rank in December 1997, and suggested that there may have been some omission of facts during this time. (``{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}''), and at the time, this great earthquake and tsunami was considered to be a ``sacred ritual caused by the wonders of the volcano.'' This is said to be because it was interpreted as being caused by the ``wrath of the mountain god.''Togo Yoshida, 1906, ``The 11th year of Jokan, the earthquake and flood of Mutsufu Castle,'' Historical Geography, Vol. 8, No. 1, pp. 1-8. It is difficult to think that Mt. Aoma exhibited some kind of divine power (volcanic activity), and the "Karita Mine" mentioned in "{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}" is attached to the summit of Zao. It seems to be referring to the vicinity of the nearby crater.Junichi Aihara 2018 "Tagajo and Jogan Tsunami" Archeology Magazine Vol. 101 No. 1, Archaeological Society of Japan, pp. 1-53. . ``Zao Daigongen Shrine'' (Okumiya) of Kattamine Shrine, which is currently enshrined on Mt. Kattadake, and later ``Zao Daigongen Otabigu Shrine'' (Satomiya Shrine) in Togatta, are not only the enshrined deity ``Zao Daigongen,'' but also ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ten Water God|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokusui God|1=UNKNOWN}}, which are positioned as the gods in charge of the water of heaven and earth throughout Mutsu Province.At the end of the Heian period, it was under the protection of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|Q3082851|WD=Q3082851}}, {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, and {{ill|lt=Oshu Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942}}, but with the fall of the Oshu Fujiwara clan, it lost its protectors, and it is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo left it ``scattered left and right with rubble, with nowhere to put its feet.''. During the year {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}}, the chief priest's distant grandson Shokan Sato had the will to restore the area, and since Nishinomiya belonged to the ancient path and was no longer accessible to local residents, he moved Nishinomiya to the northwest of Miyamura (current location). By the end of {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039}}, it was once again in ruins, and local residents were downsizing it and rebuilding it as a small shrine. {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} In 1600, Date Masamune attacked Shiroishi Castle and made Katakura Kagetsuna the lord of Shiroishi Castle. Kagetsuna designated Miyarenzo-ji Temple as his Bettoji Temple, and decided to rebuild a major shrine from the devastation of the shrine. In 1611, the second generation, Shigenaga, carried out the wishes of his predecessor, cleaning out the old shrine and renovating the various halls. He appointed Kanzai as a Sengu doshi, gave him 20 koku of temple territory, and made Bettoji Renzo-ji Temple the Katakura family's temple for the blessings of disaster relief.The current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}}3 ({{ill|lt=1718|Q6899|WD=Q6899}}) by {{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}}lord {{ill|lt=Katakura Murakyu|Q6375040|WD=Q6375040}}. ``Records of the Yamaga Clan Shinto Priests'' (formerly owned by Tomomi Sato, copied by Nobumitsu Katakura, reprinted in ``Zao Town History Materials Volume II'')) states, ``In 1988, the lord of the feudal lord, Katakura Ujimura Kyushu, built the main shrine's worship hall Nagatoko torii, and was subordinated by Lord Yoshimura, the Sakonoe Lieutenant General of the Gobukaza.. === History of company name === The company names are ``Reine no Shrine'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716) and ``Shiratori Myojin'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716). (2010), ``Katta{{Ruby|Shrine|Miya}}'' (by local people, this is called ``Kattamiya Shrine''.There is a temple called Takarachiyama Renzo-ji Temple on the south side of the shrine, and according to both legends, it is mentioned in the Shinmecho. The Karutamine Shrine in Tokoro is not actually the shrine of this shrine.On the other hand, there are doubts about the Chokan 11th year of the reign of Karutamine. It is called Mt. Fubo, and since there is Zao Gongen Shrine on the mountain, it is called Zao Dake. }} Lists the volcanic activity in Zao in 1999 (Ou Kanseimon Roshi, 1719), Kattamine Shrine (1719). ``Hounai Meisekishi'' (1741), ``Katamine Shrine'' (``Hunai Fudoki'', 1772), ``Shoichii Shiratori Daimyo Shrine'' (``Engishiki Jinmyocho Nisōdai Kō Katta District Ichizamine Shrine Nigozako. ``Okoha Okatta Yamanouchi Gojinza Ari no Koro,'' ``Yasunaga Fudoki Goyosho Deri'' (1777), June 10, 1872, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} It was listed as a local shrine, and on March 1, 1907, it was designated as a Shinto shrine and a designated shrine, and on November 9, 1928, in the Godaiten, it was listed as a prefectural shrine.Due to the defeat in the {{ill|lt=Boshin War|Q309184|WD=Q309184}}, in 1870, the former lord of Shiroishi Castle, Katakura Kojuro Kuninori, ordered over 150 of his former vassals to join him. He moved to Horobetsu District, Shunkoku, and enshrined a branch spirit of Japan Takeru, the deity enshrined at Kattamine Shrine, the general guardian deity of the Katakura clan, and enshrined it at the local Myoken Inari Shrine, making it the guardian deity of the development of Horobetsu District. In 1871, a new shrine building was erected at Hama-Nanaju, Horobetsu Village, and the company name was changed to {{ill|lt=Katta Shrine (Noboribetsu City)|Q17226399|WD=Q17226399|lt=Katta Shrine}}. ===Shrine=== *Main shrine *Temple of worship *Torii *Zuishinmon *Belfry *Kagura stage === Surroundings === * {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981}} === Shrine of the same name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Alias |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Prefecture}} !{{ill|lt=gun|Q517267|WD=Q517267}} !{{ill|lt=town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478}} !colspan="2" |district !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|lt=Miya

Prefecture}} |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Katta-gun|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|lt=Kari
田
gun}} | |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Range|Q167951|WD=Q167951|lt=Zao}}・Mt. Katta (sengu from Mt. Kumano) |A crater that displays divine power around the summit |{{Wiki coordinates|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Mt. Katta)|Q65249271|WD=Q65249271|lt=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Okumiya'''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|lt=Kura

Town}} |East foothills, {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981}} Takenobo ruins|{{Wiki coordinates|38|7|28.61|N|140|34|35.57|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Satomiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mount Aoma|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mount Okarita) |Mine Shrine, which enshrines the mountain body (Okattayama), is enshrined with Shiratori Shrine (Nihon Takeru) in Nishiyama |{{Wiki coordinates|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyojin)}} !Katamine Shrine |'''Shiratori Daimyojin'''' |} {{List of coordinates|section=Shrine of the same name}} Additionally, on the Yamagata Prefecture side, there are Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine) (Shimotakarazawa, Yamagata City), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hango, Ishidaka, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, Kaminoyama City). === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation ={{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}Ouomi Shrine Yushin Legend |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternative name = Shiratori Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=January 14th|Q2257|WD=Q2257}}, {{ill|lt=March 19th|Q2424|WD=Q2424}}, {{ill|lt=September 9th|Q2856|WD=Q2856}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Katamine Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Kattamine jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Kattamine jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Hall]]Kattamine Shrine is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874}}. It is a Shikinai shrine named {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}} in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}," and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is the largest shrine in the county, and is the general guardian of Katta County and the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Date clan|Q1151061|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date family}} vassals {{ill|lt=Katakura clan|Q15149847|WD=Q15149847|lt=Katakura family}} ({{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}} lord). Another name is ``Shiratori Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} In {{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, where our company is located, and in the neighboring {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}}, there is a ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=swan|1=UNKNOWN}} faith,'' and it is said that there are many swan {{ill|lt=votive tablets|Q687288|WD=Q687288}} dedicated to our company.[http://www.dokitan.com/sitei/25.html Kattamine Shrine Ema] (Zao Town Board of Education)[https://www.town.shibata.miyagi.jp/index.cfm/81,27038,154,279,html Shiratori Incident and the Shibata Family] (Shibata Town)``Murata Town History''. There is also a group of ancient Shiratori monuments on the grounds. For the relationship between Shiratori and Takeru Nihon, please refer to the corresponding article. === History === The auspicious origins of Kattamine ShrineRyuho Jitsumasa Yasuon Kunshu 1716 "Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Origin" (Reprinted in "Zao Town History Materials Part II" by Zao Town History Compilation Committee 1989) b>Tosayfupersons haveri'' and ``舊TOmineノShrineto號su''. At that time, Mt. Aoma was called ``Okattayama,'' and it is the origin of Kattamine Shrine. At the Zuishinmon Gate ({{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}}10 ​​(1827)), there is a vertical plaque that reads ``Mine Shrine.'' {{ill|lt=Nara Period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}}{{ill|lt=Yoro|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} From the Shiroishi side where the county residence of Katta District, founded in 722, is located. The view of Mt. Aoma is the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=twin-eared peak|1=UNKNOWN}} of ``Mt. Akera'' (820m) and ``Mt. Aoma'' (799m). It closely resembles {{ill|lt=Mt. Tsukuba|Q1754619|WD=Q1754619}} (Mt. Nyotai 877m, Mt. Nantai 871m), and the oldest belief in Kattamine deity is Mt. Aoma. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mt. Shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} Nobumitsu Katakura, 1957, “Okatta Sanroku Essays,” Sendai Local Studies Vol. 17, No. 1, pp. 52-58.In {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788}} and {{ill|lt=Katsuta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771}}, there is a ``Swan Legend'' related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}}. There are several shrines that remain, and according to old legend, when Takeru Takeru, the second son of Emperor Keiko, was on his eastern expedition, Takeru moved to Kumabono in Seishu. She died, turned into a swan and flew away, and enshrined Shiratori Myojin where she landed, or {{ill|lt=Emperor Yomei|Q349281|WD=Q349281}}'s daughter {{ill|lt=Anahobe Manjin Princess. It is said that|Q11597250|WD=Q11597250}} (mother of {{ill|lt=Prince Shotoku|Q263972|WD=Q263972}}) died in this area, turned into a swan, and the local people enshrined a shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakuma Dogan|1=UNKNOWN}}1719 "Ou Kansekimon Roshi" Volume 4. There is a legend that the second son of Japan Takeru, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}, ordered the lord of Katta County to build a shrine on the western mountain of Katta, where a swan perched..In {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, in the 20th year (801) of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}, {{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}} invaded Ezo. When they prayed for the pacification of the Toi at the Nishinomiya Shrine (enshrined deity: Nihon Takeru) during the logging, Prince Myonen Daito ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ani Ryutame|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Lord Bangu ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mothere|1=UNKNOWN}}) surrendered, and the remnants were slaughtered and released into exile. At this time, the shogun transferred Mine no Kami to the hall of the West Palace, where it was enshrined as a shrine. A small fable enshrining a wooden statue of Yakushi was built in the ruins of the Mine Shrine, and this led to the mountain being named ``Yakushigatake.''{{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} caused by the Mutsu Province Earthquake on May 26, 869, Karitamine was appointed as the Junior Fourth Rank Lower on December 8 of the same year, and again on December 25. In Sakuma Togan's 1719 ``Ou Kansekimon Roshi,'' the rank of the lower deity of Jushimi was super-rank, questioning the fact that Karita Mine no Kami was super-rank in December 1997, and suggested that there may have been some omission of facts during this time. (``{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}''), and at the time, this great earthquake and tsunami was considered to be a ``sacred ritual caused by the wonders of the volcano.'' This is said to be because it was interpreted as being caused by the ``wrath of the mountain god.''Togo Yoshida, 1906, ``The 11th year of Jokan, the earthquake and flood of Mutsufu Castle,'' Historical Geography, Vol. 8, No. 1, pp. 1-8. It is difficult to think that Mt. Aoma exhibited some kind of divine power (volcanic activity), and the "Karita Mine" mentioned in "{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}" is attached to the summit of Zao. It seems to be referring to the vicinity of the nearby crater.Junichi Aihara 2018 "Tagajo and Jogan Tsunami" Archeology Magazine Vol. 101 No. 1, Archaeological Society of Japan, pp. 1-53. . ``Zao Daigongen Shrine'' (Okumiya) of Kattamine Shrine, which is currently enshrined on Mt. Kattadake, and later ``Zao Daigongen Otabigu Shrine'' (Satomiya Shrine) in Togatta, are not only the enshrined deity ``Zao Daigongen,'' but also ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ten Water God|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokusui God|1=UNKNOWN}}, which are positioned as the gods in charge of the water of heaven and earth throughout Mutsu Province.At the end of the Heian period, it was under the protection of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|Q3082851|WD=Q3082851}}, {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, and {{ill|lt=Oshu Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942}}, but with the fall of the Oshu Fujiwara clan, it lost its protectors, and it is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo left it ``scattered left and right with rubble, with nowhere to put its feet.''. During the year {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}}, the chief priest's distant grandson Shokan Sato had the will to restore the area, and since Nishinomiya belonged to the ancient path and was no longer accessible to local residents, he moved Nishinomiya to the northwest of Miyamura (current location). By the end of {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039}}, it was once again in ruins, and local residents were downsizing it and rebuilding it as a small shrine. {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} In 1600, Date Masamune attacked Shiroishi Castle and made Katakura Kagetsuna the lord of Shiroishi Castle. Kagetsuna designated Miyarenzo-ji Temple as his Bettoji Temple, and decided to rebuild a major shrine from the devastation of the shrine. In 1611, the second generation, Shigenaga, carried out the wishes of his predecessor, cleaning out the old shrine and renovating the various halls. He appointed Kanzai as a Sengu doshi, gave him 20 koku of temple territory, and made Bettoji Renzo-ji Temple the Katakura family's temple for the blessings of disaster relief.The current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}}3 ({{ill|lt=1718|Q6899|WD=Q6899}}) by {{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902}}lord {{ill|lt=Katakura Murakyu|Q6375040|WD=Q6375040}}. ``Records of the Yamaga Clan Shinto Priests'' (formerly owned by Tomomi Sato, copied by Nobumitsu Katakura, reprinted in ``Zao Town History Materials Volume II'')) states, ``In 1988, the lord of the feudal lord, Katakura Ujimura Kyushu, built the main shrine's worship hall Nagatoko torii, and was subordinated by Lord Yoshimura, the Sakonoe Lieutenant General of the Gobukaza.. === History of company name === The company names are ``Reine no Shrine'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716) and ``Shiratori Myojin'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716). (2010), ``Katta{{Ruby|Shrine|Miya}}'' (by local people, this is called ``Kattamiya Shrine''.There is a temple called Takarachiyama Renzo-ji Temple on the south side of the shrine, and according to both legends, it is mentioned in the Shinmecho. The Karutamine Shrine in Tokoro is not actually the shrine of this shrine.On the other hand, there are doubts about the Chokan 11th year of the reign of Karutamine. It is called Mt. Fubo, and since there is Zao Gongen Shrine on the mountain, it is called Zao Dake. }} Lists the volcanic activity in Zao in 1999 (Ou Kanseimon Roshi, 1719), Kattamine Shrine (1719). ``Hounai Meisekishi'' (1741), ``Katamine Shrine'' (``Hunai Fudoki'', 1772), ``Shoichii Shiratori Daimyo Shrine'' (``Engishiki Jinmyocho Nisōdai Kō Katta District Ichizamine Shrine Nigozako. ``Okoha Okatta Yamanouchi Gojinza Ari no Koro,'' ``Yasunaga Fudoki Goyosho Deri'' (1777), June 10, 1872, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} It was listed as a local shrine, and on March 1, 1907, it was designated as a Shinto shrine and a designated shrine, and on November 9, 1928, in the Godaiten, it was listed as a prefectural shrine.Due to the defeat in the {{ill|lt=Boshin War|Q309184|WD=Q309184}}, in 1870, the former lord of Shiroishi Castle, Katakura Kojuro Kuninori, ordered over 150 of his former vassals to join him. He moved to Horobetsu District, Shunkoku, and enshrined a branch spirit of Japan Takeru, the deity enshrined at Kattamine Shrine, the general guardian deity of the Katakura clan, and enshrined it at the local Myoken Inari Shrine, making it the guardian deity of the development of Horobetsu District. In 1871, a new shrine building was erected at Hama-Nanaju, Horobetsu Village, and the company name was changed to {{ill|lt=Katta Shrine (Noboribetsu City)|Q17226399|WD=Q17226399|lt=Katta Shrine}}. ===Shrine=== *Main shrine *Temple of worship *Torii *Zuishinmon *Belfry *Kagura stage === Surroundings === * {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981}} === Shrine of the same name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Alias |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Prefecture}} !{{ill|lt=gun|Q517267|WD=Q517267}} !{{ill|lt=town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478}} !colspan="2" |district !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|lt=Miya

Daimyojin)}} !Katamine Shrine |'''Shiratori Daimyojin'''' |} {{List of coordinates|section=Shrine of the same name}} Additionally, on the Yamagata Prefecture side, there are Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine) (Shimotakarazawa, Yamagata City), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hango, Ishidaka, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, Kaminoyama City). === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === |Image = [[File:Kattamine jinja Haiden.JPG|270px|Katamine Shrine Worship Hall]]
Harmony Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}}Miyaza Baba 1 |Position = {{Coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shakaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin University|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) Ronsha
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation ={{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}Ouomi Shrine Yushin Legend |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternative name = Shiratori Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=January 14th|Q2257|WD=Q2257|1=January 14}}, {{ill|lt=March 19th|Q2424|WD=Q2424|1=March 19}}, {{ill|lt=September 9th|Q2856|WD=Q2856|1=September 9}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Katamine Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|38|3|0.9|N|140|39|10.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Kattamine jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Kattamine jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Hall]]Kattamine Shrine is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}}{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}}. It is a Shikinai shrine named {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}," and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is the largest shrine in the county, and is the general guardian of Katta County and the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Date clan|Q1151061|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date family|1=Date clan}} vassals {{ill|lt=Katakura clan|Q15149847|WD=Q15149847|lt=Katakura family|1=Katakura clan}} ({{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902|1=Shiroishi Castle}} lord). Another name is ``Shiratori Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} In {{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}}, where our company is located, and in the neighboring {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788|1=Shibata District, Miyagi}}, there is a ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=swan|1=UNKNOWN}} faith,'' and it is said that there are many swan {{ill|lt=votive tablets|Q687288|WD=Q687288|1=Ema (Shinto)}} dedicated to our company.[http://www.dokitan.com/sitei/25.html Kattamine Shrine Ema] (Zao Town Board of Education)[https://www.town.shibata.miyagi.jp/index.cfm/81,27038,154,279,html Shiratori Incident and the Shibata Family] (Shibata Town)``Murata Town History''. There is also a group of ancient Shiratori monuments on the grounds. For the relationship between Shiratori and Takeru Nihon, please refer to the corresponding article. === History === The auspicious origins of Kattamine ShrineRyuho Jitsumasa Yasuon Kunshu 1716 "Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Origin" (Reprinted in "Zao Town History Materials Part II" by Zao Town History Compilation Committee 1989) b>Tosayfupersons haveri'' and ``舊TOmineノShrineto號su''. At that time, Mt. Aoma was called ``Okattayama,'' and it is the origin of Kattamine Shrine. At the Zuishinmon Gate ({{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}10 ​​(1827)), there is a vertical plaque that reads ``Mine Shrine.'' {{ill|lt=Nara Period|Q189178|WD=Q189178|1=Nara period}}{{ill|lt=Yoro|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} From the Shiroishi side where the county residence of Katta District, founded in 722, is located. The view of Mt. Aoma is the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=twin-eared peak|1=UNKNOWN}} of ``Mt. Akera'' (820m) and ``Mt. Aoma'' (799m). It closely resembles {{ill|lt=Mt. Tsukuba|Q1754619|WD=Q1754619|1=Mount Tsukuba}} (Mt. Nyotai 877m, Mt. Nantai 871m), and the oldest belief in Kattamine deity is Mt. Aoma. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mt. Shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} Nobumitsu Katakura, 1957, “Okatta Sanroku Essays,” Sendai Local Studies Vol. 17, No. 1, pp. 52-58.In {{ill|lt=Shibata District|Q1131788|WD=Q1131788|1=Shibata District, Miyagi}} and {{ill|lt=Katsuta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}}, there is a ``Swan Legend'' related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}}. There are several shrines that remain, and according to old legend, when Takeru Takeru, the second son of Emperor Keiko, was on his eastern expedition, Takeru moved to Kumabono in Seishu. She died, turned into a swan and flew away, and enshrined Shiratori Myojin where she landed, or {{ill|lt=Emperor Yomei|Q349281|WD=Q349281|1=Emperor Yōmei}}'s daughter {{ill|lt=Anahobe Manjin Princess. It is said that|Q11597250|WD=Q11597250|1=Princess Hashihito no Anahobe}} (mother of {{ill|lt=Prince Shotoku|Q263972|WD=Q263972|1=Prince Shōtoku}}) died in this area, turned into a swan, and the local people enshrined a shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakuma Dogan|1=UNKNOWN}}1719 "Ou Kansekimon Roshi" Volume 4. There is a legend that the second son of Japan Takeru, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}, ordered the lord of Katta County to build a shrine on the western mountain of Katta, where a swan perched..In {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, in the 20th year (801) of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}}, {{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} invaded Ezo. When they prayed for the pacification of the Toi at the Nishinomiya Shrine (enshrined deity: Nihon Takeru) during the logging, Prince Myonen Daito ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ani Ryutame|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Lord Bangu ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mothere|1=UNKNOWN}}) surrendered, and the remnants were slaughtered and released into exile. At this time, the shogun transferred Mine no Kami to the hall of the West Palace, where it was enshrined as a shrine. A small fable enshrining a wooden statue of Yakushi was built in the ruins of the Mine Shrine, and this led to the mountain being named ``Yakushigatake.''{{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} caused by the Mutsu Province Earthquake on May 26, 869, Karitamine was appointed as the Junior Fourth Rank Lower on December 8 of the same year, and again on December 25. In Sakuma Togan's 1719 ``Ou Kansekimon Roshi,'' the rank of the lower deity of Jushimi was super-rank, questioning the fact that Karita Mine no Kami was super-rank in December 1997, and suggested that there may have been some omission of facts during this time. (``{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}''), and at the time, this great earthquake and tsunami was considered to be a ``sacred ritual caused by the wonders of the volcano.'' This is said to be because it was interpreted as being caused by the ``wrath of the mountain god.''Togo Yoshida, 1906, ``The 11th year of Jokan, the earthquake and flood of Mutsufu Castle,'' Historical Geography, Vol. 8, No. 1, pp. 1-8. It is difficult to think that Mt. Aoma exhibited some kind of divine power (volcanic activity), and the "Karita Mine" mentioned in "{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" is attached to the summit of Zao. It seems to be referring to the vicinity of the nearby crater.Junichi Aihara 2018 "Tagajo and Jogan Tsunami" Archeology Magazine Vol. 101 No. 1, Archaeological Society of Japan, pp. 1-53. . ``Zao Daigongen Shrine'' (Okumiya) of Kattamine Shrine, which is currently enshrined on Mt. Kattadake, and later ``Zao Daigongen Otabigu Shrine'' (Satomiya Shrine) in Togatta, are not only the enshrined deity ``Zao Daigongen,'' but also ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ten Water God|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokusui God|1=UNKNOWN}}, which are positioned as the gods in charge of the water of heaven and earth throughout Mutsu Province.At the end of the Heian period, it was under the protection of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|Q3082851|WD=Q3082851|1=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}}, {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}}, and {{ill|lt=Oshu Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942|1=Northern Fujiwara}}, but with the fall of the Oshu Fujiwara clan, it lost its protectors, and it is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo left it ``scattered left and right with rubble, with nowhere to put its feet.''. During the year {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}, the chief priest's distant grandson Shokan Sato had the will to restore the area, and since Nishinomiya belonged to the ancient path and was no longer accessible to local residents, he moved Nishinomiya to the northwest of Miyamura (current location). By the end of {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039|1=Bunroku}}, it was once again in ruins, and local residents were downsizing it and rebuilding it as a small shrine. {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} In 1600, Date Masamune attacked Shiroishi Castle and made Katakura Kagetsuna the lord of Shiroishi Castle. Kagetsuna designated Miyarenzo-ji Temple as his Bettoji Temple, and decided to rebuild a major shrine from the devastation of the shrine. In 1611, the second generation, Shigenaga, carried out the wishes of his predecessor, cleaning out the old shrine and renovating the various halls. He appointed Kanzai as a Sengu doshi, gave him 20 koku of temple territory, and made Bettoji Renzo-ji Temple the Katakura family's temple for the blessings of disaster relief.The current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}}3 ({{ill|lt=1718|Q6899|WD=Q6899|1=1718}}) by {{ill|lt=Shiraishi Castle|Q1037902|WD=Q1037902|1=Shiroishi Castle}}lord {{ill|lt=Katakura Murakyu|Q6375040|WD=Q6375040|1=Katakura Murayasu}}. ``Records of the Yamaga Clan Shinto Priests'' (formerly owned by Tomomi Sato, copied by Nobumitsu Katakura, reprinted in ``Zao Town History Materials Volume II'')) states, ``In 1988, the lord of the feudal lord, Katakura Ujimura Kyushu, built the main shrine's worship hall Nagatoko torii, and was subordinated by Lord Yoshimura, the Sakonoe Lieutenant General of the Gobukaza.. === History of company name === The company names are ``Reine no Shrine'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716) and ``Shiratori Myojin'' (``Mutsu Katta County Soja Shiratori Myojin Engiki'', 1716). (2010), ``Katta{{Ruby|Shrine|Miya}}'' (by local people, this is called ``Kattamiya Shrine''.There is a temple called Takarachiyama Renzo-ji Temple on the south side of the shrine, and according to both legends, it is mentioned in the Shinmecho. The Karutamine Shrine in Tokoro is not actually the shrine of this shrine.On the other hand, there are doubts about the Chokan 11th year of the reign of Karutamine. It is called Mt. Fubo, and since there is Zao Gongen Shrine on the mountain, it is called Zao Dake. }} Lists the volcanic activity in Zao in 1999 (Ou Kanseimon Roshi, 1719), Kattamine Shrine (1719). ``Hounai Meisekishi'' (1741), ``Katamine Shrine'' (``Hunai Fudoki'', 1772), ``Shoichii Shiratori Daimyo Shrine'' (``Engishiki Jinmyocho Nisōdai Kō Katta District Ichizamine Shrine Nigozako. ``Okoha Okatta Yamanouchi Gojinza Ari no Koro,'' ``Yasunaga Fudoki Goyosho Deri'' (1777), June 10, 1872, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} It was listed as a local shrine, and on March 1, 1907, it was designated as a Shinto shrine and a designated shrine, and on November 9, 1928, in the Godaiten, it was listed as a prefectural shrine.Due to the defeat in the {{ill|lt=Boshin War|Q309184|WD=Q309184|1=Boshin War}}, in 1870, the former lord of Shiroishi Castle, Katakura Kojuro Kuninori, ordered over 150 of his former vassals to join him. He moved to Horobetsu District, Shunkoku, and enshrined a branch spirit of Japan Takeru, the deity enshrined at Kattamine Shrine, the general guardian deity of the Katakura clan, and enshrined it at the local Myoken Inari Shrine, making it the guardian deity of the development of Horobetsu District. In 1871, a new shrine building was erected at Hama-Nanaju, Horobetsu Village, and the company name was changed to {{ill|lt=Katta Shrine (Noboribetsu City)|Q17226399|WD=Q17226399|lt=Katta Shrine|1=Katta Shrine}}. ===Shrine=== *Main shrine *Temple of worship *Torii *Zuishinmon *Belfry *Kagura stage === Surroundings === * {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} === Shrine of the same name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Alias |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Prefecture|1=Prefectures of Japan}} !{{ill|lt=gun|Q517267|WD=Q517267|1=Commandery (China)}} !{{ill|lt=town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478|1=Towns of Japan}} !colspan="2" |district !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|lt=Miya

Kumano) |A crater that displays divine power around the summit |{{Wiki coordinates|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Mt. Katta)|Q65249271|WD=Q65249271|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine (Katta-dake)}} |'''Okumiya'''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|lt=Kura

Town|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} |East foothills, {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} Takenobo ruins|{{Wiki coordinates|38|7|28.61|N|140|34|35.57|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Satomiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mount Aoma|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mount Okarita) |Mine Shrine, which enshrines the mountain body (Okattayama), is enshrined with Shiratori Shrine (Nihon Takeru) in Nishiyama |{{Wiki coordinates|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|Map|name=Katamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyojin)}} !Katamine Shrine |'''Shiratori Daimyojin'''' |} {{List of coordinates|section=Shrine of the same name}} Additionally, on the Yamagata Prefecture side, there are Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine) (Shimotakarazawa, Yamagata City), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hango, Ishidaka, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, Kaminoyama City). === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Kattamine-jinja (Miya, Zao)}} * {{ill|lt=Shiratori
